actions.thought: I am on the button in a three-handed game with Ace-Seven offsuit. This is a very strong position to open, and A7o is well within my opening range for this format. Raising allows me to take initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or play with the advantage of position in the hand. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened to 250. Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand and does not play well out of position. It is standard to fold this hand against a raise from the button.

K8o is a marginal offsuit hand. Facing a 2.5x raise from the Button, playing out of position with a hand easily dominated (by better Kings or better eights) is not profitable. Folding is the best play here to preserve the stack for a better opportunity.

K8 suited on the button in a 3-handed game is a strong hand that should be played aggressively. I will open for a standard raise of 2.5x the big blind to exert pressure on the blinds and utilize my positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

Facing a 2.5bb raise from the Button, holding an offsuit connector (89o) in the Small Blind is too weak to defend. Playing this hand out of position will lead to negative implied odds and difficult post-flop spots, so the optimal choice is to simply fold.

97 offsuit is a bottom-tier hand to defend with, even from the Big Blind. Given the wide range an opponent plays from the Button, I am still at a significant equity disadvantage. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for better spots in this tournament.

We are on the Button in a 3-handed game. King-Six suited is a strong enough hand to open-raise and apply pressure on the blinds. A standard 2.5x raise to 250 is ideal to steal the pot or play in position post-flop if called.

Qh 9c is a marginal, offsuit hand. Defending the small blind against a button raise with this hand puts me in a disadvantageous position for the rest of the hand. It is safer and more disciplined to fold and wait for a better spot to enter the pot.

I am in the big blind holding 2c 7h, which is arguably the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. The player on the button (Pro) raised to 250, and the small blind folded. To call, I would need to put in 150 more chips. Although the pot odds are mathematically tempting (150 to win a total pot of 550), the hand has very poor equity and extremely low playability post-flop, especially out of position against a button open. I should save my chips for a better spot.

I am on the button with suited cards (Jd 6d), which provides reasonable playability in a 3-handed game. Opening from the button is the standard strategy to attempt to steal the blinds and take the initiative. I will raise to 250, consistent with the table's established opening size, to put pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 6c, a weak offsuit hand. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a raise to 250. Given that I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack early in the tournament.

A2s is a standard defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. It has good playability, can make the nut flush, and plays reasonably well post-flop. Getting good pot odds (150 to win 400), calling is a solid play to realize equity without bloating the pot out of position.

I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser with Ace high and no draws on a board that hits their range well (Kd Tc 3d). The standard play is to check to the raiser and evaluate their action, though I'll likely fold to any bet.

The board texture of Tc Kd 3d is relatively dry but hits a pre-flop raiser's range well. I have a flush draw and overcards, providing reasonable equity. I will make a continuation bet of 200 into the 550 pot to maintain initiative, test the opponent's strength, and potentially take down the pot now given their check.

I have Ace-high with a weak kicker and no direct or backdoor draws on a board that connects heavily with the pre-flop raiser's range. Facing a continuation bet, I have practically zero equity and folding is the only viable option.

I am on the button with Jack-Three offsuit. While it is a three-handed game and ranges are wider, this hand is significantly below the typical opening range for the button. Given the stacks and the stage of the tournament, there is no reason to play such a weak hand out of position post-flop if the blinds defend. I will fold.

I have 45o in the Small Blind. This is a very weak, unsuited connector and I will be out of position post-flop against the Big Blind. Flashlight has shown some aggression in previous hands, so trying to steal the pot with a weak hand here isn't worth the risk. I will fold and conserve my stack.

I am on the Button with 8d 2d, a very weak and heavily dominated hand. Even though it is suited, it lacks playability, high card strength, or connectivity. This is an easy pre-flop fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Holding Ace-Ten offsuit in a heads-up situation after the button folds is a strong holding. I am in the small blind, which is the dealer position in 3-handed play. I will raise to 300 chips to exert pressure on the big blind and potentially take down the pot immediately or control the hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 300. In a blind-versus-blind scenario, an Ace has significant value, and I will have positional advantage for the rest of the hand. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600, giving me 3:1 pot odds (25% equity required). Ace-Trey offsuit typically has much more than 25% equity against a standard Small Blind opening range. I will call to see the flop and play in position.

I have flopped top two pair with Ace-Ten on a board of Ad-Ts-8s. This is an extremely strong hand, although the board presents some flush and straight draw possibilities. I want to build the pot for value against weaker Aces or pairs like 8-x, while simultaneously charging any draws (like spades or J-9) to see the next card. A bet of 350 into the 600 chip pot is a balanced size that extracts value and provides poor pot odds for my opponent.

I hit top pair on the flop, but my kicker is very weak (3). The opponent's bet of 350 into 600 is a standard continuation bet on an Ace-high board. While I could be out-kicked by any other Ace in their range (AJ, AQ, etc.), folding top pair here is too weak. Raising would likely only get called by better hands and force out bluffs I'm currently beating. Calling allows me to control the pot size and see how the turn develops, especially considering the flush and straight draws present on the board.

I have flopped top two pair with Aces and Tens on an Ad-Ts-8s-6h board, which remains an extremely strong hand. The turn card (6h) is relatively safe and does not complete any obvious draws. My opponent called my flop bet, indicating they likely have a piece of the board (a weaker Ace, a draw, or a mid-pair). I will continue to bet for value to build the pot and charge for potential draws, sizing my bet to approximately 60% of the pot to maintain a favorable risk-reward ratio while keeping the opponent in with weaker hands.

I have top pair on an Ace-high board in a blind-versus-blind confrontation. Although my kicker is the weakest possible (3), folding top pair here would be overly exploitable, as Flashlight's range for betting the turn includes many semi-bluffs like flush draws (spades) and straight draws (QJ, 97). The 6h on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. I have the required pot odds to call (approximately 27.6% equity needed), and being in position allows me to see how the river develops. Raising would only isolate me against better Aces or two-pair hands, so I will call to keep the pot controlled and potentially reach a cheap showdown.

The river 4h missed all potential flush and straight draws, meaning my top two pair (Ace-Ten) is extremely likely to be the best hand. Given that my opponent has called both the flop and the turn, they are likely holding a weaker Ace, a pair of Tens, or potentially a mid-pair they are unwilling to fold. Betting 1200 into the 2900 pot is a solid value bet that extracts chips from this range while remaining a reasonable price for the opponent to call.

I am facing a third barrel from Flashlight on a board of Ad-Ts-8s-6h-4h. My hand, Ace-Trey, is a pure bluff-catcher here because any other Ace in my opponent's range has me out-kicked or has made two pair (A6, A4, A8, AT). However, the price to call is 1200 into a total pot of 4100, meaning I only need to be right about 22.6% of the time. In a blind-versus-blind confrontation, Flashlight's range includes many missed draws such as spades (KsQs, QsJs, etc.) or straight draws like QJ. The river bet sizing (roughly 41% of the pot) is smaller relative to previous streets, which can sometimes indicate a polarized range or a thin value bet. Given the pot odds and the wide ranges inherent in BvB play, I will call to see the showdown.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with T9o. This is a playable hand in late position, and raising allows me to contest the blinds or potentially take control of the pot post-flop. A raise to 250 is consistent with my previous opening sizing and provides enough pressure to the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. The Button has raised to 250. Queen-Six offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position, especially against an active opponent with a chip lead. Folding is the standard professional play here to avoid unnecessary losses and wait for a stronger starting hand.

Facing a button raise from Flashlight with A2o in the Big Blind. A2o is a weak offsuit Ace that plays very poorly out of position. Even though I am getting decent pot odds, the reverse implied odds and lack of post-flop playability make this a clear fold. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.
I am on the button with KQo, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I will open with a standard raise to 2.5x the big blind to take initiative and build the pot while in position.

87 offsuit is too weak to play from the small blind facing a button open. We will be out of position for the rest of the hand against the original raiser, and the big blind is still left to act behind us. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h-Td. The button has opened for 250. Given the pot odds of 150 to win 400, calling to defend my big blind is a standard decision. I will see a flop and evaluate the board texture before proceeding, as 9-10 offsuit has decent connectivity.

I am in the big blind with bottom pair (9s) on a King-high board. Being out of position against the button, this is a clear check. The board is dry, and my hand lacks significant strength against the opponent's likely range of broadway cards or higher pairs. I will check to see the opponent's intention and likely fold to any significant aggression.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a dry board (K-3-9). Flashlight checked to me, and as the pre-flop aggressor, I should place a continuation bet for value. I expect to be called by weaker Kings, 9x hands, or middle pocket pairs. A bet of roughly half the pot (250 into 550) is standard here to build the pot while protecting my hand.

The opponent has made a continuation bet of 250 into an 800 pot. My pair of nines is weak on this King-high board, but the pot odds are favorable (calling 250 to win a potential 1050). Folding now would be overly conservative against a wide button opening range, as I still have equity against various overcards and bluffs the opponent might be playing. I will call to see the turn card and re-evaluate based on the next card and the opponent's subsequent action.

I am out of position in the Big Blind with middle pair (nines) on a board of Kc-3d-9s-2d. The board is relatively dry, and my hand has some showdown value but is vulnerable to any overcards (like a King or better) and aggressive betting from the button. Checking is the most standard and disciplined play, allowing me to control the pot size, evaluate the opponent's response, and potentially reach a cheap showdown. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or cause the opponent to fold their bluffs, effectively turning my pair into a bluff, which is not an ideal strategy.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (Kd Qh) on a very dry board (Kc 3d 9s 2d). Flashlight has check-called the flop and checked the turn, which suggests a marginal made hand like a weaker King, a Nine, or a pocket pair. The deuce of diamonds is a brick that doesn't change the board state significantly. I will bet again for value to extract chips from those weaker hands and to protect my equity against any potential diamond draws that picked up equity on the turn.

I am facing a bet of 550 into a pot of 1600. My middle pair (nines) on a relatively dry board (Kc-3d-9s-2d) has reasonable showdown value. Folding would be too conservative against a button-opening range that could easily include weaker pairs, straight draws (like 54, though unlikely given the action), or pure bluffs. Calling allows me to reach the river at a reasonable price, controlling the pot size while keeping my range wide enough to not be easily pushed around.

I am in the Big Blind, and the action has checked to me on the river. The board is relatively dry (Kc-3d-9s-2d-6h), and I have a pair of nines. This hand has some showdown value but is essentially a bluff-catcher against a button-opening range. Betting for value is unlikely to yield results as I would primarily only get called by hands that beat me. Therefore, checking is the optimal strategy to reach showdown as cheaply as possible.

I have top pair with a queen kicker (Kd Qh) on a very safe board (Kc 3d 9s 2d 6h). Flashlight has check-called the flop and turn, indicating they likely have a marginal made hand like a weaker King (KT, KJ) or a Nine. Since the river is a complete brick, I will bet for value to extract one last street of chips from those weaker hands. A bet of 1000 into a 2150 pot is a standard value sizing that targets their calling range.

I am facing a bet of 1000 into a pot of 2150. My pair of nines is a bluff-catcher on this King-high board. While the opponent's line (betting three streets) strongly suggests a King or a better pocket pair, they could also be overplaying missed draws or weaker pairs from the button. Given the pot odds of roughly 3.15:1, I only need to be correct approximately 24% of the time to make this a profitable call. Considering the wide range of an opponent on the button, calling to keep them honest is the mathematically optimal play rather than folding too easily.

With pocket 4s on the Button, an open raise is standard to steal the blinds or take the initiative into a post-flop pot where we will have position. Opening to 2.5x (250) is a solid sizing given the stack sizes (~95 BBs effective). If we get 3-bet, we can evaluate whether we are getting odds to set-mine depending on the size, but for now, raising is better than limping or folding.

Holding King-Eight suited in the small blind against a button open is a marginal situation. Playing out of position with this hand is difficult, as it is easily dominated by better Kings. Given the stack depths and the fact that the big blind is still to act, folding is the most disciplined and profitable play to avoid tough post-flop decisions OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 6h. The Button (Pro) has raised to 2.5x (250). In a 3-handed game, defending the Big Blind requires a wide range, but 8-6 offsuit is near the bottom of that range and difficult to realize equity with out of position. Given the 2.5x sizing and my position, folding is the standard disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
I am holding a very weak hand, 3s 4c, on the button. There is no strategic value in playing this hand against two opponents, so I will fold to conserve my stack.

With the button folding, I am in the small blind with 85s. This is a reasonable hand to attempt a steal or play a pot against the big blind. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the hand, while still having a hand that can flop well if called.
Facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind, holding an unsuited, disconnected, and weak hand like Ten-Six offsuit, the clear action is to fold. The hand lacks both raw equity and playability post-flop.

I am on the button with 8d 5c, which is a very weak starting hand. While button opens can be wide in 3-handed play, 85o is near the bottom of any opening range and doesn't play well post-flop. Flashlight in the big blind has shown a willingness to defend and call down, making a steal attempt with such a weak hand less profitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

With the action folded to me in the Small Blind, A6o is a strong enough hand to raise and attempt to steal the Big Blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on Flashlight to defend with marginal hands, and if called, we still hold decent high-card equity.

7-9 offsuit is a weak hand with limited playability, especially out of position. Defending the big blind with this hand against an open raise from the small blind is generally negative EV given the stack depth. Folding is the disciplined play here to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Q6o is a weak offsuit hand. Even on the button as first to act, it does not warrant an open raise because of its poor playability post-flop and lack of high-card strength. I will fold and wait for better starting hands.

With the button folding, I am in a heads-up situation from the small blind. Queen-Five suited is a decent hand to open with in this position. Raising to 250 puts pressure on the big blind and allows me to take control of the hand or potentially steal the pot immediately.

I am in the big blind with 7d 3d, which is a weak suited gapper. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 250, making it 150 more to call. Given that Flashlight has been quite active and has a larger stack, 73s is generally too weak to defend even against a small blind open, especially out of position if we were heads-up (though technically I'm in position on the SB, the BB/SB dynamic still favors folding such low-equity trash hands). I've observed Flashlight playing aggressively in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack for better spots.

Q2 offsuit is an extremely weak hand and does not justify an investment of 100 chips to see a flop, especially in a 3-handed game where position is helpful but hand strength remains paramount. It is mathematically and strategically sound to fold and preserve my stack for a more advantageous hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Qh7d. Since the Button (Flashlight) has folded, I am head-to-head with the Big Blind (Pro). In Hand 10, I successfully took the pot from the Small Blind with a similar 3x raise. Q7o is a reasonable hand to open for a raise in a blind-versus-blind situation to put pressure on the Big Blind and attempt to win the blinds and antes immediately.

We are facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind and hold Q4o in the Big Blind. This hand is too weak and disconnected to defend, even with position. The best action is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Qc 3s. In a three-handed game, while the button opening range is wider than in full-ring, Q3 offsuit is still significantly below the threshold for a profitable opening raise. Given that we are still early in the tournament (Hand 14 of 150) and have healthy stacks, there is no need to play such a marginal hand out of position post-flop or risk a 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Ace-Jack suited is a premium hand in a Blind vs Blind scenario after the Button folds. Raising to 3x the big blind (300 chips) builds the pot with a strong hand, prevents the Big Blind from realizing their equity for free, and establishes control heading into the post-flop play.

I am in the big blind with 6s8s facing a raise from the small blind. This is a classic spot to defend as 6s8s is a suited connector with decent playability post-flop. The pot odds (200 to win 400) offer a clear incentive to call, and playing in position as the BB allows me to realize my equity against the SB's opening range.

With the nut flush draw and an overcard (Ace) on a Qh 7h 5s flop, I have a very strong semi-bluffing hand. As the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to apply pressure, fold out missed hands, and build the pot in case I hit my flush.

The flop is Qh-7h-5s, and I hold 6s-8s. This gives me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 4 or 9). With 300 chips required to call into a 600 pot, the 3:1 pot odds provide a mathematically sound justification for a call. I will continue to see the turn, maintaining pot control while realizing my equity.

With the nut flush draw, a gutshot straight draw, and an overcard on the turn, my equity remains extremely high. Firing a second barrel applies fold equity against marginal pairs and weaker draws, while building the pot in case I hit one of my numerous outs on the river.

I am facing a bet of 600 into a pot of 1200, needing to call 600 to see the river. With 6s8s on a board of Qh-7h-5s-Td, I have an open-ended straight draw with 8 clean outs (any 9 or 4). While my immediate pot odds (25%) are slightly better than my mathematical probability of hitting on the river (approx 18%), the implied odds are significant because Pro is playing aggressively and leading the betting. Calling here allows me to realize my equity and potentially win a larger pot if I hit my straight, making it a mathematically and strategically sound decision.

I have rivered the Broadway straight (Ten to Ace), which is the absolute nuts on this unpaired, no-flush board. Since my opponent has called my bets on both the flop and the turn, they likely hold a strong made hand such as two pair, a set, a strong top pair (like AK or KQ), or perhaps a lesser straight like J9. I want to extract maximum value from these second-best hands, so I will make a value bet of around 75% of the pot.

I missed my open-ended straight draw and backdoor flush draw. With high-card 8 on a board of Qh-7h-5s-Td-Ks, I have zero showdown value and cannot beat any part of the opponent's betting range. Calling here is mathematically incorrect as I have no equity to improve, so folding is the only rational action.

Qd2s is a very weak starting hand, often considered a 'trash' hand. Even though I am on the button and can steal blinds, Q2o has very poor equity and playability post-flop. There is no justification for opening with this hand, so the standard and mathematically correct play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the small blind with 2h 3c, which is a very weak starting hand with little potential for post-flop value. Since the button folded, I have the option to complete the blind by calling 50, but given the trash quality of my cards and the disadvantage of being out of position, the most disciplined and profitable long-term decision is to fold and preserve my stack.

8-10 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability, even from the button. In a three-handed game, while position is valuable, this hand lacks the strength or potential to justify an open-raise against two opponents who have been reasonably active. It is best to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am in the Small Blind with Q3o. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, so the action is on me. My opponent in the Big Blind is 'Pro', who has been playing relatively aggressively but has also shown a willingness to fold when facing significant pressure (as seen in Hand #14). While Q3o is a marginal hand, in a blind-vs-blind scenario, it has some merit as a steal attempt. However, calling the extra 50 to see a flop out of position is generally a losing play with this hand strength. Raising to steal is an option, but given the tournament status and the fact that I want to maintain a solid image, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot, as Q3o performs poorly post-flop when called.

I am on the Button with Ten-Deuce offsuit (Td 2s). While button ranges in 3-handed play should be wide, this hand is at the bottom of the distribution and does not warrant an open against active opponents like Pro and Flashlight. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

With 9s 4s in the Small Blind, the hand is too weak and disconnected to play profitably out of position against the Big Blind, even as a steal. The best decision is to fold and preserve the stack for a better opportunity.

Holding 2-3 offsuit (2h 3d) pre-flop. This is arguably the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Even with position on the Button, this hand is completely unplayable and the only logical action is to fold immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 6c, which is a very weak hand with almost no equity. Even though the button has folded and I have the option to limp or raise, there is no strategic value in playing this hand against the Big Blind. Folding is the standard and correct play here to minimize losses and wait for better opportunities.

My hand, 3s 4d, is extremely weak and lacks any significant playability or equity. Even in position on the button, there is no strategic justification for entering this pot with such a poor holding. Folding is the standard and correct play.

I am in the Small Blind with KTo. Since the Button has folded, it is down to me and the Big Blind. KTo is a very strong hand in a heads-up blind-versus-blind situation. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it immediately or build value against the Big Blind's range.

My hand is 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The Small Blind raised to 300, and despite getting pot odds, my hand has virtually no post-flop playability or equity. Folding is the only reasonable choice here.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium starting hand in three-handed play. Standard strategy dictates an opening raise to take control of the pot and leverage position. I will raise to 300, which is three times the big blind, to narrow the field or win the blinds and antes immediately.

I am in the Small Blind with J4 offsuit. The Button raised to 300. This is a very weak hand to play, especially out of position and against a raise. Folding is the standard and mathematically correct play here to preserve stack size.

I am in the big blind with QTo against a button raise. This is a standard defending hand that plays well enough to see a flop, especially given the favorable pot odds. Calling is the most balanced strategy here, keeping the pot manageable and allowing me to evaluate the flop relative to the opponent's likely range, rather than bloating the pot unnecessarily with a 3-bet.

I missed the flop completely with my Queen-Ten high. Being out of position against the button, checking is the standard play to re-evaluate the opponent's continuation bet. I have overcards, but they are not strong enough to lead out, so I will check to see how the opponent proceeds.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-Queen. The flop of Jack-Six-Five rainbow is relatively dry. Since Flashlight checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of roughly half the pot to maintain my aggression, fold out air, and potentially get value from weaker hands or draws while holding two overcards.

The board (5d-Js-6c) is relatively dry, and my holding of Queen-Ten is currently just queen-high with two overcards. While the pot odds of 300 into 1000 are decent, I am out of position against the button, and my hand lacks significant equity against a standard C-bet range. Most of the opponent's opening range contains pairs or high cards that currently beat me. Folding here avoids getting stuck in a tough spot out of position with a marginal hand and preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the Button in a 3-handed game with K9 offsuit. This is a very standard spot to open-raise and attempt to steal the blinds. A raise to 2.5x the big blind puts pressure on the Small Blind and Big Blind.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Eight suited, which is a hand with decent equity and playability against a Button open. While playing out of position post-flop is a disadvantage, the pot odds (calling 200 to win 400 total) are favorable. Calling is the most balanced and efficient way to continue without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a potentially strong range.

I am in the Big Blind holding 97 offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 250, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has called. This creates a multi-way pot where I would be out of position against both opponents for the remainder of the hand. Although the pot odds are 4:1 (150 to call into a pot of 600), 97 offsuit is a very weak hand that realizes equity poorly and is often dominated by the ranges of the other players. Given the deep stacks and the disadvantageous position, folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack for higher-equity opportunities.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a board that is paired with Queens and Tens. My hand (King-high) has no made pair or strong equity, making it a clear candidate for a check. Leading out into the button would be ill-advised given the board texture and my lack of a strong range, so checking to the aggressor to evaluate their c-betting action is the standard and most strategic play.

The flop of Qs Tc Td pairs the board with high cards, which strongly favors my pre-flop button opening range. Flashlight's check allows me to take the betting lead. With King high and a gutshot straight draw (any Jack gives me a straight), I have decent equity even if called. A small continuation bet of about one-third the pot (200 chips) should be enough to fold out Flashlight's missed broadway cards, weak Ax, and small pocket pairs.

The board is paired with Tens, which aligns well with the button's opening range (containing many Broadway cards and pairs). My King-high has very little showdown value and minimal drawing equity. Calling a bet out of position on this texture is likely to result in a difficult turn decision and continued loss of chips. I will fold to preserve my stack for a more advantageous situation.
With Ace-Eight suited on the button, I have a strong hand for 3-handed play. Raising to 250 is a standard move to take control of the pot, build value, and potentially steal the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Five offsuit. Flashlight has raised from the Button. Queen-Five is a weak hand to play out of position, even against a standard button opening range. Given the stack depths and my position, it is a clear fold to avoid playing a marginal hand out of position for the rest of the hand.
84o is a very weak, disconnected, and unsuited hand with practically zero playability out of position. Facing a button raise, folding is the only standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 7d 6d, which is a strong speculative hand in a three-handed game. My position allows me to play aggressively. Based on my previous play style, raising to 300 is a standard move to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play with initiative in position.
Jack-Nine offsuit is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Facing a 3x raise from the button, the best play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the button to 300. The pot currently contains 450 chips, and I am required to put in 200 to call. Given the pot odds and the fact that I am in position for the post-flop play, defending with an Ace-high hand is standard practice. Calling allows me to see a flop with a reasonable hand and keep the pot manageable.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a King-high board. My hand (Ace-high) is weak and offers little showdown value without improvement. A check is the standard play here to keep the pot small and evaluate the opponent's aggression on this board.

I have a very strong drawing hand with an open-ended straight draw (3 or 8) and a flush draw (any diamond), giving me roughly 15 outs. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Flashlight checked to me on a King-high board, a continuation bet is the standard play. This allows me to take the pot down immediately if my opponent missed, or build a larger pot for when I hit one of my many outs.

I am in the big blind out of position against a button raise on a King-high board with two diamonds.
