I hold a pair of deuces with an Ace kicker on a 6h-Kc-Tc-2h board. Flash checked the flop, suggesting they do not have a strong hand, and their turn bet of 400 into a 600 pot is small. With this pot size and the relative weakness of the board, my pair of deuces has sufficient showdown value to justify a call to see the river, keeping the pot manageable while potentially inducing further bluffs.

I hold a pair of deuces on a board of 6h-Kc-Tc-2h-5d. My hand has very low showdown value, and it is highly unlikely to win against any pair of 5s, 6s, 10s, or Kings. Since I was the one who called the turn bet, the most prudent action is to check, aiming to get to showdown as cheaply as possible without committing further chips into a potentially lost pot.

I have a pair of Tens with a Queen kicker on a board of 6h-Kc-Tc-2h-5d. On the turn, I bet 400 and Flashlight called, suggesting they have a piece of the board or a draw. The river 5d is a blank that doesn't complete the primary draws (clubs or straight draws). Flashlight has checked to me on the river. While my hand is relatively strong in a three-handed game, it serves well as a showdown value hand. Betting might only get called by a King or a better Ten, while weaker hands like a Six or missed draws will likely fold. By checking, I can safely realize my equity and win the pot against their missed draws and weaker pairs without risking a check-raise.

Pocket Tens is a premium starting hand. From the Button, raising is the standard play to build the pot, take initiative, and prevent the blinds from seeing a cheap flop. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind.

I am in the small blind with Q6 offsuit, a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. With Pro raising from the button, calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop against an opponent with a larger stack. Given my current stack size, it is better to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7s 8h. The Button (Pro) raised to 250, and the Small Blind folded. I need to call 150 more into a pot that will be 550 total (250 from Pro, 50 from SB, 100 from BB, 150 call), which gives me favorable pot odds of about 2.67:1. Given that 7-8 offsuit has decent playability and I am closing the pre-flop action with a large stack, I will call to see a flop.

I have completely missed this flop with my 7-8 offsuit. The board is Queen-high and doesn't offer any straight or flush draws for my specific cards. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) and likely fold if they make a continuation bet, as I have no real equity in this pot.

I have second pair on a board with one overcard (the Queen) and a flush draw present. A continuation bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 the pot is standard here to extract value from worse pairs, straight draws, and flush draws, while also denying free equity to hands with a King or Ace. Having the Ten of Diamonds is an added bonus, as it blocks some diamond draws and provides backdoor flush equity.

I have completely missed the flop with 7-high and have no draws or potential to improve significantly. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet on a Queen-high board. Without any equity or a viable bluffing opportunity, folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

With QTo on the button, I have a playable hand that allows me to open the pot and apply pressure on the blinds. My stack is becoming relatively short compared to the other players, so I need to be more aggressive to accumulate chips. A standard open-raise to 2.5x the big blind is the optimal strategy here to either steal the blinds or take the initiative in the hand with position.

I am holding a low pocket pair (33) in the Small Blind. In a 3-handed game, pocket pairs have decent value, but 33 is primarily a set-mining hand. Flashlight, the button, has raised to 2.5x. With a stack of 149.5 BB, I have plenty of room to call and see a flop. Raising (3-betting) here is an option to isolate, but it puts me in a tough spot if Flashlight (who is relatively short-stacked at 44.5 BB) decides to shove. Calling allows me to see a flop for a relatively low price and evaluate based on whether I hit a set or face further aggression.

Q9o is too weak of a hand to play out of position against a button open and a small blind flat call. It has poor equity against their ranges and significant reverse implied odds if I make a pair. Folding is the standard and optimal play to preserve my stack.

On this flop of Ace-high and Queen-high, my pocket threes have very little value unless I hit a set, which I didn't. I am out of position against Flashlight, who raised pre-flop and likely has a range that connects well with these high cards. I will check and evaluate based on Flashlight's action, but I am prepared to fold to any significant aggression.

The flop contains an Ace, which is a card that hits the small blind's calling range significantly harder than mine. My pair of Queens is a marginal holding here, and betting would likely force out hands I'm beating while getting called by hands that have me crushed, such as any Ace or better. Checking back allows me to maintain pot control and evaluate the turn card, keeping my stack preservation in mind given my relatively shorter position.

I have turned a set of threes (3h 3d) on a board of Qs Ad 6h 3c. Pre-flop, Flashlight raised from the button and I called from the small blind. On the flop, we both checked, which suggests Flashlight might have a marginal made hand or air. Now that I have a monster hand, I need to build the pot. A bet of 400 into the 600 pot is a strong value bet that can be called by an Ace, a Queen, or even a six, while also potentially inducing a raise if Flashlight was slow-playing a bigger hand or decides to bluff.

Flash checked the flop, which might indicate they didn't hit the Ace. Their turn lead of 400 into a 1000 pot could be a probe bet with a weaker hand, a draw, or a cautious bet with an Ace. Given that I have middle pair with a Queen, calling is a high-value play that allows me to see the river with favorable pot odds while maintaining control of my remaining stack. Folding here would be premature against this sizing.

I have a full house (threes full of aces) on a board of Qs-Ad-6h-3c-Ah. After Flashlight checked the flop, my turn bet of 400 was called, suggesting he has a piece of the board, likely a Queen, a pocket pair, or possibly a slow-played Ace. The river Ace is an excellent card for me because it likely gives Flashlight three-of-a-kind if he has any Ace in his hand, and my full house beats all of his possible three-of-a-kind and two-pair combinations (like Qx or 6x). I only lose to rare hands like QQ, 66, AQ, or A6, most of which would have likely bet the flop. I will bet 900 for value, targeting a call from an Ace or a Queen.

The board texture is Qs Ad 6h 3c Ah. My pair of Queens is now essentially a bluff catcher. Flash's line of checking the flop and then betting on both the turn and river, especially when an Ace pairs the board, strongly indicates they have an Ace in their range. Betting into this board on the river strongly suggests value, and calling with second pair here is risky and unlikely to be profitable long-term against the range of hands Flash is betting. I will fold to preserve my remaining stack.

I am on the button with 3c 5d, which is a very weak hand. While I have the chip lead and button position is advantageous for stealing blinds, 35 offsuit is near the bottom of my range. Flashlight is relatively short-stacked and might be looking for a spot to shove or defend aggressively. There is no need to play such a weak hand in this spot.

With A6o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise and put pressure on the Big Blind, who is playing somewhat tight. A raise to 3x (300) is standard to steal the pot or play heads-up with a decent starting hand.

I hold pocket aces, the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Since the opponent in the small blind raised to 300 and the button folded, I am heads-up. With 3,950 chips behind, I should play aggressively to maximize value. A 3-bet to 1,000 is a standard size that builds a significant pot while allowing me to evaluate my opponent's reaction.

I opened A6o from the Small Blind as a standard steal attempt, but the Big Blind 3-bet to 1000. Calling here puts me out of position with a weak, easily dominated Ace, and I am not getting the right odds or implied odds to continue. 4-betting essentially commits us for his whole stack, which is far too risky with A6o. Folding is the disciplined and optimal decision.
I am dealt 7-4 offsuit on the button, which is a very weak and unprofitable hand to play, even in late position. Folding is the only reasonable decision here.

With 8s 5s in the small blind and the button having folded, this is a prime opportunity to raise and attempt to steal the big blind. The hand has decent potential to connect with the board, and raising provides better initiative than simply limping or folding.

K8o is a solid hand to defend the Big Blind with against a Small Blind raise in a short-handed game. I have position on Flashlight for the rest of the hand and I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win a 350 pot). Folding is too tight, and while 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and play my hand profitably in position against his likely wide range.

The flop of 6h-Td-2h misses my hand completely, and holding 8s-5s offers only a weak backdoor flush draw. Since I am out of position, betting into the big blind without significant equity is risky. Checking is the standard play to pot control and evaluate the turn, planning to fold if my opponent bets strongly.

I have King-high on a Ten-high board. Flashlight, the pre-flop aggressor, has checked. While I could bet to try and take the pot down, checking back allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card for free in position. My King-high has some showdown value against his missed overcards, and I avoid potentially getting check-raised by a short stack.

I have 8-high on a board of 6h-Td-2h-2d. My hand has no showdown value and no significant draws. Since the opponent checked behind on the flop, their range is wide, but there's no reason for me to put more chips in the pot with this holding. Checking is the best move to maintain pot control; if the opponent bets on the turn or river, I can easily fold.

Flashlight has checked for the second time on the turn. While my King-high (Kc 8h) is unlikely to be the best hand right now, it still possesses some showdown value against his missed overcards (like QJ, Q9, etc.). By checking back, I control the size of the pot and guarantee myself a free river card in position. Betting might force a fold from weaker hands, but it also risks a check-raise from a short-stacked opponent who might be trapping or deciding to shove with any piece of the board. I prefer to see the river and re-evaluate.

I hold 8-high with 8s-5s on a board of 6h-Td-2h-2d-4h. My hand has no showdown value, as even a missed pair of 5s would likely lose here. Betting would be a pure bluff without any blockers to the flush or strong hands, and given that the pot is small and we have checked down every street, there is no reason to inflate the pot now. Checking is the only logical move to get to showdown, accepting that I likely lose the hand.

The board is 6h-Td-2h-2d-4h and my opponent (Flashlight) has checked on every street after raising pre-flop. My King-high (Kc 8h) has a reasonable amount of showdown value against a small blind opening range that includes many missed overcards like QJ, Q9, or J9. While I could bet to try and push him off an Ace-high hand, checking back is the most prudent play. It allows me to reach showdown for free in a small pot where I likely have the best hand or am losing to a marginal hand that won't fold anyway. I will check and take my chances at winning the pot with King-high.

I am holding 3d 4s, which is an extremely weak hand with no showdown value or significant equity. Given my stack size of 41 BBs, there is no pressure to play marginal hands, and playing this trash hand from the button would be a negative expected value move. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 5h, a very weak starting hand. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. While it only costs 50 more to call, playing a hand as weak as J5o out of position against a competent opponent (Pro) is generally a losing play. Raising is an option to steal the blind, but given the stack sizes and the trashy nature of the hand, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am on the Button with Jack-Seven offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a marginal hand that sits right on the edge of an opening range. Given my significant chip lead and the stack sizes of my opponents—especially Flashlight, who is down to 40 big blinds and may look to jam over a late-position raise—I choose to play conservatively. Pro has also shown a willingness to three-bet from the blinds in previous hands. Folding here allows me to maintain my lead and wait for a more advantageous spot without committing chips with a weak hand that has poor playability post-flop.
35o is an extremely weak hand. With the action folded to me in the small blind, there is no reason to put more chips into the pot. Folding is the only sensible play here.

Q4o is a very weak offsuit hand. Even from the button, attempting a steal is unprofitable here, especially against a shorter stack (Flashlight) who could easily shove, and a deep stack (Flash) who can defend wide. The most standard and optimal play is to fold.

The hand 2h3s is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Being in the small blind means I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against the big blind (Flash). Given my stack size and the poor quality of these cards, folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to conserve my chips for a more favorable situation.

As the button with Queen-Jack offsuit, this is a strong hand to open with in a late position. Given the stacks and the goal to maximize value, raising to 300 is a standard move to either steal the blinds or build a pot in position.

I am holding 4s 2c in the Small Blind, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Flashlight has raised from the button to 300. Given the worthlessness of my hand and being out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no justification for calling or raising. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

K8o is a weak offsuit hand. Facing a 3x open from the button, it is not strong enough to defend from the big blind. Folding is the standard play to avoid marginal post-flop situations out of position.

I am on the button with T9o, which is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a 3-handed game. I want to apply pressure to the blinds, especially the short-stacked Flashlight (BB). Raising to 300 is consistent with standard opening sizes and allows me to play the pot in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with K3o, which is a very weak, easily dominated hand. The Button (Flash) has opened to 3 big blinds. Playing this hand out of position against a pre-flop raiser is a losing proposition, and it does not play well enough to justify a 3-bet bluff. The best and most standard move here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Holding 6-3 offsuit is a bottom-tier starting hand. Despite the pot odds offered, I am out of position against a button raiser with a large stack. Investing chips into this pot with such a weak range is negative expected value, especially given my relatively short stack. Folding is the most disciplined choice.
Q2s is a very weak hand even from the button. While stealing is an option, the hand lacks playability if called, and it's better to save chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. With 150 chips already in the pot and 50 required to call to see the flop, it is mathematically and strategically incorrect to invest further in this hand out of position against the Big Blind, who holds a significant chip lead.

J4 offsuit is a very weak hand with low equity. Despite being on the button, it is not worth investing chips, especially as a short stack. I need to preserve my remaining 4100 chips for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. The button has folded. While a steal attempt is possible, Ten-Seven offsuit is a relatively weak hand to play out of position against 'Pro' in the Big Blind, who has shown a willingness to defend or three-bet in previous hands. Given my substantial chip lead, I prefer to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better opportunity rather than risking chips in a potentially difficult post-flop situation.

I am currently the chip leader with 16,100 chips. Holding Queen-Four offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a marginal spot. While opening wide from the button is a standard strategy, Q4o is at the bottom of the range and doesn't play well post-flop, especially against Pro and Flashlight who have shown they are willing to defend or 3-bet. Given my stack advantage, I prefer to fold this weak hand and wait for better opportunities to apply pressure.

75o is a very weak hand, an unsuited one-gapper with no high-card value. Playing this from the small blind out of position against the big blind is not profitable. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

With pocket nines on the button, I have a strong starting hand that plays well post-flop, but it's also strong enough to build a pot right now or pick up the blinds. A standard raise to 2.5x the big blind (250 chips) is a solid play to extract value and thin the field.

I am in the small blind with Ace-Nine offsuit against the button's open. The pot is offering me 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400), making a call mathematically sound. While I am out of position, A9o is strong enough to defend here rather than folding. A 3-bet could isolate but might force me to play a bloated pot out of position with a marginal holding, so I will call and see a flop.

I am holding AT-offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a strong hand in 3-handed play. The Button opened with a standard raise, and the Small Blind, who is relatively short-stacked (39bb), just called. This call often indicates a capped or wide range. By raising (squeezing) here, I can either take down a 6bb pot immediately or isolate one of the players while maintaining the initiative. Given my chip lead, I want to apply pressure on the shorter stacks.

Facing a 3-bet to 1000 from the Big Blind after the Small Blind called my initial raise, I have to decide how to proceed with pocket nines. Since my effective stack against the 3-bettor is about 9500 and it costs me 750 more to call, I am getting roughly 12-to-1 implied odds, which is sufficient for set-mining. Furthermore, I will have position post-flop. A 4-bet would over-leverage my hand and force a fold to a jam, while folding immediately sacrifices a strong pair and good implied odds. Therefore, flat calling to see a flop is the most profitable decision.

The situation has drastically changed since my initial decision to call the open. With the big blind squeezing to 1000 and the original raiser calling, my Ace-Nine offsuit is significantly disadvantaged against two opponents who have shown strength. Calling would commit nearly 20% of my remaining stack to a marginal hand out of position. The risk of being dominated by the big blind's squeezing range and the original raiser's call is too high. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound decision to preserve my stack for a better spot.

As the pre-flop 3-better, the King-high rainbow board (K-7-3) is very favorable for my perceived range, which includes hands like AA, AK, and KK. Although I missed with Ace-Ten, a continuation bet here allows me to maintain the initiative and likely forces folds from hands like middle pocket pairs (88-JJ) or other unpaired overcards that the Button (Pro) might have called with pre-flop. Given the dry texture of the board, a small bet of around 1/3 pot is sufficient to put pressure on his range without risking too many chips if he has a King.

The flop brings a King, which heavily hits Flash's 3-betting range (like AK, KQ) and his likely overpairs (AA, QQ, JJ, TT) all have me beat. Facing a continuation bet of 800 into a 2250 pot, my pocket nines are essentially reduced to a weak bluff catcher. Since I lack any meaningful draws and my opponent's perceived range is very strong, the most disciplined and +EV play is to fold and preserve my stack.

King-Four offsuit is a weak hand even from the button position, especially with my current stack of 3900 (39 big blinds). Given the aggressive nature of the opponents, it is better to fold and wait for a more premium hand to commit chips rather than playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 7c, a solid suited connector. Since the Button has folded, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation against Pro. Given my significant chip lead and the strength of my hand in this specific scenario, I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on the Big Blind.

Facing a 3x open from the Small Blind, King-Five suited is strong enough to defend from the Big Blind. We will have positional advantage post-flop and the hand has decent playability with flush and straight potential, as well as high card strength.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop has come Ace-high. This board texture strongly favors my perceived range as the raiser. I have flopped middle pair (Sevens) with a Nine kicker. Betting here serves as a continuation bet to represent the Ace and pick up the pot immediately against hands that missed. It also provides some protection and value against draws or smaller pairs that Pro might be holding. A half-pot bet of 300 is standard in this spot to maintain initiative.

With the Ace of Hearts on the board and holding the King of Hearts, I have the nut flush draw. Facing a standard half-pot continuation bet from the Small Blind, I have excellent equity and the advantage of position. Calling is the most profitable play here, as it allows me to realize my equity, keeps the pot size manageable, and keeps his bluffs in the hand.

On the turn, the 2d is a relatively blank card. I have middle pair (7s) with a 9 kicker. While I represented the Ace on the flop with a continuation bet, Pro's call suggests he either has a piece of the board (possibly an Ace, a 7, or a 4) or a draw (like hearts). By checking here, I am looking to control the pot size and potentially induce a bluff from missed draws or weaker hands. It also allows me to reach the river more cheaply if I am currently behind a better hand like a weak Ace.

My opponent checked the turn, showing weakness. I hold the nut flush draw along with a gutshot to a wheel. This is an excellent spot to semi-bluff. Betting here allows me to win the pot uncontested against hands like underpairs or weak broadways, while building the pot if I hit one of my many outs.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on an Ace-high board. After I checked the turn to control the pot, Pro bet 600 into a 1200 pot (offering me 3:1 pot odds). While the Ace is a scare card, Pro could be betting with a variety of hands, including heart draws, a lower pair (like a 4), or even a total bluff after I showed weakness. My hand is too strong to fold to a single half-pot bet in a blind-vs-blind battle, but raising would likely only get called by better hands. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see the river to re-evaluate.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on an Ace-high board that now has a four-card straight (A-2-3-4) showing, meaning any 5 makes a straight. I check-called the turn to control the pot and evaluate the river. Leading out now doesn't accomplish much, as better hands (Aces, straights) will call or raise, and worse hands will likely fold. By checking, I can potentially get to showdown for free if Pro checks back with a marginal hand or a missed draw, or I can decide whether to bluff-catch if Pro bets.

The river brings the 3d, completing my gutshot straight draw to a wheel (A-2-3-4-5). Since my heart flush draw missed, my opponent may easily put me on a busted flush draw and call a reasonable bet with a pair of Aces or a stubborn middle pair. A half-pot value bet is perfectly sized to extract maximum value from these bluff-catchers without over-committing in the rare case they hold 6-5.

On the river, the board completes a four-card straight (A-2-3-4), meaning any five makes a straight. Although I have middle pair (Sevens), Pro has shown significant aggression by betting both the turn and the river on an Ace-high board. After I checked the turn and the river, my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher. Pro's range for defending the Big Blind and calling a flop continuation bet includes many Aces, small pocket pairs that may have improved, or suited connectors like 5-6. Given the board texture and Pro's double-barrel, it is highly likely I am beaten by an Ace, a straight, or even two pair. As the chip leader, I prefer to fold and preserve my stack rather than making a high-risk hero call in this spot.

I am on the button with J4 offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. Even though I have a significant chip lead, there is little incentive to enter the pot with such a low-equity hand against a short-stacked Big Blind and a solid Small Blind. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

I have 85s in the small blind and it folds to me. While it's blind vs blind, 85s is a relatively weak and disconnected suited hand. Playing out of position against the Big Blind with a marginal holding is generally unprofitable. I'll fold and look for a better spot to attack the blinds or play a pot.

4c 2s is one of the weakest starting hands in No-Limit Texas Hold'em. Despite having position on the button, this hand has practically no playability post-flop, no high card value, and no suited potential. Folding is the only strategically sound option.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-King suited, a premium hand. With the Button (Pro) having folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind (Flash). Raising is the optimal play to take initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or gain value with a dominant hand. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 92o, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Flashlight, who is the short stack, raised from the Small Blind. Although I have a significant chip lead and am getting decent pot odds (200 to win 400), defending with such a weak hand against a player who might be looking to commit their small stack is not worth the risk. I will fold and wait for a better hand to utilize my chip lead.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game. My stack is the shortest at 4050, so I need to be active to stay competitive. Jack-Seven suited is a playable hand in this position for a steal attempt. A raise to 250 is a standard size to put pressure on the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Ad8d, a premium hand in 3-handed play. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 250. Flashlight is the short stack with 3,800 chips. 3-betting here is the standard aggressive play to take control of the pot, isolate the button, and put pressure on the Big Blind. Ace-Eight suited performs well against a standard button opening range. I will raise to 850 to either win the pot immediately or narrow the field and play a heads-up pot with initiative.

Facing an open from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, King-Queen offsuit is a marginal hand that plays poorly in 3-bet pots, especially when facing strong ranges. There is a high risk of reverse implied odds against hands like AK, AQ, or big pocket pairs. Folding is the most disciplined and profitable play here.

I opened on the button with J7s and was 3-bet by the small blind. I have position and the pot odds are acceptable to continue. Given my stack depth and the ability to outplay the opponent post-flop, calling is the most balanced strategic decision to see the flop.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a very coordinated board (Td-As-Qh). This board hits a large portion of a range that calls a 3-bet pre-flop, including straights (KJ), two pairs (AQ, AT, QT), and better Aces (AK, AJ). With the opponent's stack being relatively short (SPR of 1.77), a bet here effectively commits me to the hand. To control the pot and avoid doubling up the short stack with a marginal kicker, I will check. This allows me to see how the opponent reacts and potentially induce a bluff from weaker hands or draws while keeping the pot manageable.

The opponent 3-bet pre-flop, which typically indicates a strong range including high pairs or high cards. This board of Ace-Queen-Ten is very favorable for their 3-betting range. By checking, the opponent might be trapping with a set or Top Pair, or they could be giving up with smaller pairs that missed the board. Given my short stack and the current pot size, betting would likely commit me to a risky spot against a potentially strong hand. Checking back is the most prudent decision, as it allows me to realize my gutshot straight draw equity for free and preserves my remaining stack.

The turn 4h is a blank. I checked the flop to control the pot with my top pair/weak kicker. Flashlight's check-back on the flop reinforces that they might not have a powerhouse, but could be holding a draw or a marginal piece of the board. I will check again to continue controlling the size of the pot and potentially induce a bluff or a bet from a weaker hand that I can then call. This keeps my risk low against the short stack while still giving me a chance to win at showdown or see a safe river.

The board is [Td, As, Qh, 4h] and I hold [7c, Jc]. I have a gutshot straight draw, needing a King to complete the straight. My opponent (Flash) 3-bet me pre-flop, which indicates a strong range that connects well with this board. Since Flash checked to me on the turn, I will check behind to control the pot size and preserve my short stack, while realizing my equity for free in case a King hits the river.

The river 4c is a blank. I have top pair with an 8 kicker on a board that is very coordinated (As-Qh-Td). My opponent has checked back on both the flop and the turn, which suggests they likely have a marginal made hand (like a Queen or a Ten) or a missed draw (like KJ). While I might have the best hand, my kicker is not strong enough to bet for value against an opponent who has already shown reluctance to put more chips in the pot. Checking is the safest way to reach showdown and win against their weaker range, or potentially catch a bluff from a missed draw.

The river action has gone check-check. I hold Jack-high on an Ace-Queen-Ten board with paired fours. My hand has no showdown value, and my opponent's 3-bet pre-flop range strongly connects with this board (containing many Aces, Queens, and Tens). Betting into this range with J-high would be a pure bluff that is unlikely to succeed given the board texture. Checking behind allows me to showdown my hand, minimizing risk while accepting the likely loss to a better high-card hand.

I have Ace-Six offsuit (Ac 6h) on the button. The small blind (Pro) has about 97.5 big blinds and the big blind (Flashlight) is very short-stacked with only 31 big blinds. In a 3-handed game, Ace-high hands are quite strong on the button. I will raise to 300, which is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the blinds, particularly the short-stacked Flashlight, while allowing me to fold comfortably if one of them moves all-in with a significantly better range.
Q5 offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the small blind against a button raise. Folding is the standard and correct play here.
With a very weak hand (8-3 offsuit) in the big blind, there is insufficient equity to call the raise, especially as a short stack. Folding is the disciplined play here.

With Q9o on the Button in a 3-handed game, the hand is marginal. Given that the Small Blind is short-stacked (which increases the likelihood of a re-steal shove) and the Big Blind has a very large stack that can apply significant pressure, the most prudent and low-variance play is to simply fold and look for a better spot to invest chips.

With a weak hand like [3c, Th] in the small blind, the pot is small and the opponent is in the big blind. Completing the blind to see a flop heads-up is a low-risk strategy to potentially find a favorable board, as folding would just surrender the small blind without a fight, although I must remain cautious given my stack size.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 5d. The Button has folded and the Small Blind has completed. With a weak hand like 8-high, there is no reason to raise and build a pot, especially given that I can see the flop for free by checking. I will check to see the flop.

I am in the small blind with a very weak hand consisting of Ten-high on a board of Queen-Four-Six with two spades. I have no pair, no draw, and no showdown value. Checking is the only sensible move to keep the pot small and potentially see a free card, as betting would be a pure bluff into a board that likely connected with the Big Blind's checking range.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 5d. Pre-flop was a limped pot between me and the Small Blind (Flashlight). The flop is 4s Qc 6s, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Flashlight has checked to me. Given the small pot size and my weak draw, checking back to see a free turn card is the most sensible play. There is no need to bet and risk a check-raise from a short stack when I have some equity to improve.

I am in the small blind with a strong open-ended straight draw (3-4-5-6) on a board of 4s-Qc-6s-5h. With the straight draw present, checking might allow the Big Blind to take the initiative or see a free river card. Betting allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately if my opponent doesn't have a made hand, or build a pot for when I hit my straight. Given the small pot size, a bet of 100 represents a standard continuation or semi-bluff to leverage the draw.

On the turn, the 5h gives me a pair of fives and keeps my gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) alive. Flashlight has led out with a small bet of 100 into a 200-chip pot. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the fact that I have some showdown value now with a pair, plus the potential to improve to a straight on the river, calling is the most appropriate move. Raising is unnecessary as my hand is relatively weak, and folding would be too tight given the small bet size and my current equity.

My open-ended straight draw (3-4-5-6) missed on the river, as the board of 4s-Qc-6s-5h-As did not pair or complete the straight, and the Ace of Spades arrived, which also brings a potential flush. I have only Ace-high with no showdown value, and betting into an opponent who likely has at least a pair given their previous calling action is futile. Checking is the only safe option to hopefully see a showdown cheaply or fold if my opponent makes a significant bet.

I have a pair of fives (8s 5d) on a board of 4s-Qc-6s-5h-As. The river brought both a third spade and an Ace, both of which are scare cards. Flashlight limped the small blind, checked the flop, bet small on the turn when I paired my five, and has now checked the river. My pair of fives has some showdown value in a small, limped pot, but it is unlikely to get called by any worse hands if I bet. By checking, I can see the showdown for free and potentially win against missed draws or lower pairs. There is no reason to turn my hand into a bluff or value bet here.

With a stack of 2900 (29 big blinds), I am currently the short stack at the table. While 3s 6s is a suited connector, it is far too weak to play from the button, even as a blind steal attempt against two significantly larger stacks. Preserving my stack to wait for a stronger opening hand or a better re-stealing opportunity is the most disciplined approach.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has folded. While it only costs 50 more to call and see a flop, T4o is an extremely weak hand to play out of position against Pro (the Big Blind), who has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Given my chip lead, there is no need to engage in a marginal or losing situation with a sub-optimal hand. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play to maintain my stack for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 6s7c. While this is a marginal hand, it is a standard opening range on the button to attempt to steal the blinds, especially as the chip leader. Flashlight is quite short-stacked at 28BB, and Pro has a medium stack. A raise here puts pressure on both. If Flashlight shoves, I can easily fold, and if Pro calls, I will have positional advantage post-flop.
8s 5h is a very weak, unsuited, and disconnected hand. Facing a 3x raise from the button, there is no justification to get involved from the small blind. This is a simple fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit against a button raise. My stack is currently 2800, which is approximately 28 big blinds. Ace-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend in the Big Blind against a button open. Given the pot odds, calling is the most efficient way to continue while keeping my stack intact for the post-flop play, as shoving 28BB here might be over-leveraging this specific hand.

The flop is extremely wet with three clubs, and I hold no clubs in my hand, making my Ace-high hand very weak and vulnerable. As the out-of-position player, the standard play is to check and evaluate the opponent's aggression. I have no incentive to bet into this board with no equity, and a check keeps the pot controlled.

Flashlight checked on a monotone board of clubs. I have 6s 7c with no clubs, meaning I have zero equity and no blockers to the flush. While a c-bet could represent a hand, Flashlight is short-stacked and likely to call with any club or any piece of the board. Checking behind allows me to see a free turn and potentially minimize losses with a hand that has no showdown value currently.

The board is 4c, Qc, 2c, 4s. I have Ad, 9s. We have checked down to the turn, which is a fairly passive line on a paired, wet board. My Ace-high has very little showdown value, but in a checked-down pot, checking the river is the standard play to realize my equity and get to showdown cheaply without risking more chips on a bluff that is unlikely to work against a passive opponent.

On this turn, the 4s pairs the board. Flashlight has checked for the second time. While I have the 7 of clubs, it is only a low draw to a flush on a board that already has three clubs. With 7-high, I have no showdown value, but Flashlight is short-stacked (26BB) and could be trapping or simply has a hand that won't fold to a single bet on this texture. By checking behind, I see a free river and avoid inflating the pot with a hand that has nearly zero equity if called or raised. This allows me to potentially catch a 6 or 7 on the river for minimal showdown value or just give up the hand cheaply.

The hand has been checked through the flop and turn, and now we are at the river with a board that includes a 3, 4, 4, Q, 2. My Ace-high is relatively weak but serves as a bluff catcher. Since the opponent checked behind on the flop and turn, they are unlikely to have a strong hand that they would have bet for value. Betting now would turn my hand into a bluff, which is unlikely to fold out better hands and unnecessary given the potential to win at showdown with Ace-high against the opponent's missed draws or weak pairs. Therefore, checking is the optimal play to realize my showdown equity.

The board is 4c Qc 2c 4s 3h. I have 7-high, which has virtually no showdown value. Flashlight has checked every street, indicating a weak range. However, Flashlight is also playing a short stack (26BB). While a small bet might occasionally force a fold from other 7-high or 6-high hands, most hands in Flashlight's range that checked to the river (like Ace-high or a small pair) will likely call a small bet given the pot odds and their stack size. Since I am the chip leader and this is a small pot, there is no need to risk chips on a low-equity bluff against a short stack who may feel priced into a call.

65o is a very weak, offsuit connecting hand. Even from the button, it lacks the necessary equity and playability to justify opening, especially with a deep-stacked aggressive player in the big blind. Folding is the standard and safest play.

I am in the small blind with a weak hand (2c, Td). With the button having folded, I only need to call 50 to see a flop against the big blind. However, the hand strength is insufficient to justify investing more chips, even for a completion. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

I am on the button with 32 big blinds, which is a short stack compared to my opponents. I need to play aggressively to increase my stack and steal the blinds. Jh-9s is a decent speculative hand in position, so a standard open-raise of 3x the big blind is the best play to exert pressure and potentially win the pot immediately or see a favorable flop.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Six offsuit. Flashlight, the Button, has raised to 300. Flashlight is currently the short stack with only 2900 chips (29 BB). While K6o has some value in 3-handed play, calling out of position against a short stack who is frequently opening is suboptimal. I would rather wait for a better hand or a more favorable position to accumulate chips, especially since I have a significant chip lead over both opponents.

With Pocket Queens in the Big Blind facing a Button open, we have a premium starting hand. Flashlight started with a ~32bb stack and opened to 3bb. I want to 3-bet to build the pot and extract value. A 3-bet to 900 puts maximum pressure on Flashlight, likely forcing them to either fold or commit the rest of their stack, which we are more than happy to call.

I opened the pot with J9o, which is a standard steal attempt from the button. However, facing a 3-bet from the big blind (Pro), my hand is not strong enough to continue. Calling would commit a significant portion of my remaining 29bb stack with a dominated hand, and 4-betting as a bluff is high variance. Folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with King-four suited in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. Flashlight is the short stack with only 28BB, and I have a significant chip lead. I will open for 3x the big blind to 300.

KQo is a strong hand in the Small Blind facing a Button open. A 3-bet puts maximum pressure on the Button's wide opening range and discourages the short-stacked Big Blind from squeezing or overcalling. If we face a 4-bet, we can easily fold, but against calls, we have a strong hand that dominates many broadway combinations.

I am in the big blind with 4-6 offsuit, which is an extremely weak hand. Facing an initial raise from the button and a 3-bet from the small blind, my hand has very little equity against their likely ranges. With a short stack of only 2800, it is crucial to preserve my chips for a stronger starting hand rather than calling a 3-bet with junk cards that will be difficult to play post-flop.

I am on the button with Kc4c and have been 3-bet by Pro in the small blind. Pro has shown a tendency to 3-bet frequently in recent hands (e.g., Hand #28, #34, #35, #67). My hand, while not premium, is suited and has a high card, providing decent post-flop playability. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (700 to win a 2100 total pot) and I will have the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. Given my deep stack and the favorable odds, calling to see a flop is the most profitable play here.

I missed the flop completely and have no backdoor flush draw with my KQ without a diamond. The board is paired and dry aside from the diamond draw, meaning my opponent's pre-flop calling range (which includes pocket pairs, big Aces, and suited broadways) either hit this flop, has a strong draw, or has me out-kicked with Ace-high. Since I have very little equity when called and lack backdoor equity to barrel future streets, checking and being ready to fold to a bet is the safest and most optimal play.

I am in position against the pre-flop aggressor (Pro) who 3-bet me and has now checked on a 9-9-2 rainbow board. This board is relatively dry. While I could bet to try and take the pot immediately, my King-high has some showdown value and I have a backdoor flush draw. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card, keep the pot manageable, and potentially catch Pro's bluffs or see if they continue to show weakness on the next street. It also avoids a check-raise from a trapping hand like an overpair or a 9.

The turn card is the Ace of Diamonds, completing the flush draw and hitting many broadway combinations in Flash's range. I have King-Queen high with no diamond, leaving me with almost zero equity against a calling range. Since Flash checked back the flop, his range includes Ax hands, pocket pairs, and flush draws, all of which connect heavily with this turn card. Checking and preparing to fold to any bet is the safest and most optimal play here.

Pro, the pre-flop 3-bettor, has checked for the second time on a board that now includes an Ace. In a 3-bet pot, an Ace is typically a very good card for the aggressor's range, so Pro checking here suggests either extreme weakness or a potential trap with a hand like a Nine or a slow-played Ace. Since I have King-high with no real draw and limited showdown value, I have the option to bluff, but checking back is the safer play to keep the pot controlled. It allows me to see a free river card and potentially win the pot if it goes to showdown against a missed draw or a smaller high-card hand, while also avoiding a check-raise from a trapping hand. I will check and evaluate on the river.

The river is a blank 5s. With the action checking through on both the flop and the turn, my opponent's range likely consists of marginal made hands like middle pocket pairs, or missed broadway/draw combinations. Although my King-Queen high loses to any pair, bluffing here is risky; my line of checking the flop and turn does not convincingly represent a strong hand like an Ace or a flush, making it highly possible that a middle pair calls a river bet. The safest and most conservative play to preserve my stack is to check, either winning at showdown against worse missed broadways or conceding the pot.

On the river, the board is 9d-9c-2d-Ad-5s. Pro (the pre-flop 3-bettor) has checked for the third consecutive street. While an Ace is typically a good card for a 3-bettor's range, Pro's repeated checking suggests either a total miss (like KQ, QJ, JT) or a very cautious marginal hand (like 77-JJ) that is afraid of the Ace or the paired board. My King-high has a small amount of showdown value against pure bluffs/missed broadways, but it loses to any pair. While I could attempt a bluff to represent the Ace or a Nine, the pot is already decent sized and Pro has shown in previous hands (like Hand #27) a tendency to check down medium-strength hands. Checking back is the most prudent play to see a cheap showdown, as betting might force out hands I already beat while getting called by everything that beats me.

With King-Queen offsuit on the Button in a 3-handed game, I have a very strong starting hand. Raising to 2.5x the big blind is a standard open to take the initiative, pressure the blinds, and set up a profitable situation to play in position post-flop.

I am in the small blind with a weak starting hand (Ten-Four suited). I am out of position against a raise from the button, and my stack of 2750 (27.5 big blinds) requires disciplined play to preserve capital. Calling is not profitable given the poor hand strength and positional disadvantage. Folding is the most prudent action.

I am in the big blind with 2c 4h, a very weak hand. Pro has raised from the button to 250. Given my hand is near the bottom of my range and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no reason to defend this. Folding is the most prudent play.

I am on the button with suited connectors (Jack-Nine of Diamonds), which are strong enough to open from this position. Given my short stack relative to the opponents, I need to take initiative to either win the blinds immediately or put myself in a position to win the pot post-flop. Raising to 250 is a standard size that allows me to represent strength and control the pot.

I am in the Small Blind with 45 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Flashlight, on the button, has raised to 250. Although Flashlight is relatively short-stacked and aggressive from late positions, 45o does not play well out of position and has very little equity against a standard opening range. Additionally, the Big Blind (Pro) is still to act and could 3-bet or call, putting me in a difficult spot. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve my stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s7c, which is a weak offsuit connector. Flashlight, who has a short stack of 25 BBs, has opened from the Button to 2.5 BBs. Out of position with a marginal holding against a short stack's range, the best and most standard play is to fold.

J7o is a marginal hand that sits at the bottom of a potential opening range from the button. Given Flashlight's short stack of 28BB, they are in a prime position to shove over an opening raise. Additionally, Pro has shown a willingness to 3-bet from the blinds recently. With the chip lead, there's no need to play a weak hand out of position or face a potential shove. I will fold.

K8o is a solid hand to attempt a steal from the small blind when the action folds to us. Flashlight has around 28 BBs, which is a prime re-steal stack, so I want to open to a standard 2.5x to apply pressure while minimizing risk in case he decides to 3-bet jam. If he calls, we have a King and can often navigate post-flop decently.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand (Q3 offsuit). While the pot odds of 2.3:1 are tempting, playing out of position against an aggressive opponent with a significantly larger stack makes this a losing proposition in the long run. Given my short stack size (2800 chips), I need to preserve my stack for a better spot to double up or build momentum, rather than chasing with poor cards.

54o is a very weak and unplayable starting hand. Being on the button does not justify trying to steal the blinds with this holding, especially with a short-stacked opponent in the Small Blind who could easily shove, and a deep-stacked opponent in the Big Blind. Folding is the best and standard play.

I am in the small blind and the button (Pro) has folded. With King-Jack offsuit, this is a strong enough hand to attack the big blind. Raising to 300 is a standard sizing to put pressure on the big blind and potentially take down the pot pre-flop, given my stack size of 27.5 BBs.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 6c. Flashlight, the small blind, has raised to 300. Flashlight is currently the short stack with only 2,500 chips (25 BB). While I have position on Flashlight post-flop, Qh 6c is a weak hand that does not play well against a range that likely includes many better Queens, Kings, and Aces. Given the stack sizes, Flashlight may be looking to commit chips or protect their stack. I have a healthy chip lead and don't need to defend with marginal hands here.

I am the short stack at 2900 chips (29 big blinds). On the button, I need to pick up pots to maintain my stack. Although 7c-Tc is a marginal suited connector, it is acceptable for a button steal attempt to pick up the blinds. I will open raise to 250.

I am holding Ace-Queen offsuit in the small blind, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. The button (Flashlight), who is the short stack with only 2650 chips, has raised to 250. This is a clear 3-betting situation to both extract value and isolate the short stack. By raising here, I force the Big Blind (Pro) into a tough spot and put Flashlight in a position where they may have to commit their remaining stack with a wider range of hands. A raise to 850 is a standard sizing to achieve these goals.

I am in the Big Blind with K9 suited. The Button opened and the Small Blind 3-bet to 850. K9s is too weak to call a 3-bet out of position, especially with the original raiser still to act behind me. The Button has a short stack (2650) and could easily 4-bet shove. Folding is the safest and most standard play.

I raised on the button with a speculative hand and faced a 3-bet from the small blind. With a short stack of 2650, committing 600 more to call a 3-bet with 7c-Tc is poor pot odds and high variance. I should conserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with 4s 2d, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have a significant chip lead, Flashlight in the Big Blind is short-stacked with only 25.5 big blinds, making them more likely to defend or jam. Pro in the Small Blind also has a substantial stack. There is no reason to get involved with the worst possible hand in this spot. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play.

With A8 suited in the small blind against the big blind after the button folds, I have a strong starting hand. Raising to 3 bb puts pressure on the big blind who has a relatively short stack of 25.5 bb, aiming to win the pot pre-flop or play a heads-up pot with the initiative.

I am the short stack with 2550 chips. My hole cards, Queen of Spades and Deuce of Spades, are weak for defending against a raise from the small blind. Being out of position with such a marginal hand makes it difficult to navigate post-flop. Given my limited stack, I need to preserve my remaining chips and wait for a more advantageous spot to double up rather than bleeding chips with a weak holding.

With Queen-Nine suited on the button, I have a decent hand to open for a standard raise. Flashlight in the small blind is relatively short-stacked (25 BBs), and I can easily fold if he jams. Flash in the big blind is deep, but Q9s plays reasonably well post-flop in position. I'll open to 2.5x.

I am holding 7c-2d, which is statistically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Despite the pot odds offered, playing this hand out of position against a raise from the button is fundamentally incorrect. With a short stack of 2500, I need to exercise discipline and wait for a more playable hand to attempt to double up.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit. The Button (Pro) has opened with a raise to 250. Flashlight has folded from the Small Blind. I need to call 150 more to see the flop, and the pot is currently 400. With pot odds of approximately 27%, A3o is well within the defending range against a standard Button opening range. I will call to see the flop and play from there.

I have Ace-high on a board that contains two broadway cards (King and Jack), which generally favors the range of the pre-flop aggressor. Since I am out of position and have not improved my hand, the most standard and prudent play is to check to the Button and see how they respond.

I flopped a very strong semi-bluffing hand with a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw on a Kd-Jd-4s board. Flash has checked to me. A continuation bet is optimal here to build the pot in case I hit my draw and to apply pressure, potentially winning the pot immediately if Flash has completely missed or holds a weak pair. A bet size of slightly under half-pot will accomplish this efficiently.

I have Ace-high on a board of King-Jack-Four. This board strongly favors the pre-flop raiser's range (the Button). I checked, and Pro made a standard continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 550. While the pot odds are roughly 24%, my hand has very little equity against a range that includes many Kings, Jacks, and pocket pairs. Without a pair or a significant draw, and being out of position, I have no reason to continue in this hand. Folding is the most disciplined and standard professional play here.

With a stack of 2500 (25 big blinds) and a weak hand like 3-10 offsuit, I do not have a strong enough hand to contest the pot, even in the button position. It is better to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable opportunity to enter the pot.

With Flashlight folding on the button, I am in a blind-vs-blind situation against Pro. 8h 5h is a decent hand to open in this spot to apply pressure and attempt to win the blinds immediately. Given my chip lead, a standard 3x raise is appropriate here to define the hand and take the initiative.

We hold K8o in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Flash) raised to 3x the big blind. While we will have position post-flop, K8o is a weak offsuit king that suffers from reverse implied odds and is frequently dominated by the SB's raising range. Calling here leads to difficult post-flop decisions, and there are much better hands to use as 3-bet bluffs. Folding is the most solid and standard choice.

I am currently the chip leader with nearly 16,000 chips. Holding Ten-Six offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is marginal. While opening to steal blinds is a common play, the Big Blind (Flashlight) is relatively short-stacked with only 24 BBs (2,400 chips), making them more likely to shove over a raise. I prefer to wait for a stronger hand to exert pressure rather than risking chips with a weak holding against a short stack's potential desperation play.

With Q6o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I am facing the Big Blind who has a relatively short stack of 24 BBs. Q6o is a very weak hand to play out of position, and raising risks a 3-bet jam that I cannot call. Calling (limping) is also sub-optimal with a hand this weak out of position. The most fundamentally sound decision is to fold.
74o is a very weak, unplayable starting hand. Even on the button, this hand has terrible equity and poor playability post-flop. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the small blind with K-10 offsuit, which is a strong hand heads-up against the big blind. With my stack being short compared to the other players, I need to be aggressive to accumulate chips. Raising is more effective than limping here to either take down the blinds immediately or play a hand with the initiative.

I hold King-Jack suited in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind situation. Flashlight, the Small Blind, has a relatively short stack of approximately 23 big blinds and has min-raised to 200. I will 3-bet to 600 to exert pressure and take control of the pot. Given my positional advantage and the strength of my hand against a wide Small Blind opening range, this raise allows me to extract value or potentially win the pot immediately.

I am short-stacked with only 23.5 big blinds remaining, which necessitates a more aggressive strategy to build my stack. Flash (the Big Blind) has demonstrated an aggressive 3-betting style throughout the tournament, and their 3-bet here could easily be a wide range of holdings. K-10 offsuit is a strong enough hand in this heads-up scenario to push all-in. Calling 400 chips would leave me with only 1750 behind and force me to play a pot out of position, which is suboptimal. By shoving, I maximize my fold equity against Flash's bluffs or marginal hands, and I am reasonably live if called.

I started the hand with KJs in the Big Blind. After Flashlight (Small Blind) min-raised to 200, I 3-bet to 600. Flashlight has now 4-bet shoved for 2350 total (approximately 23.5 big blinds). The pot is currently 2950, and I need to call 1750. This gives me pot odds of 1750 / 4700, which is approximately 37.2%. KJs is a premium hand in a blind-vs-blind battle. Against a typical short-stack shoving range that includes pairs, Ace-highs, and other Broadways, KJs usually has between 40-50% equity. Since I only need roughly 37% equity to break even on the call, and my stack of 15,350 can easily absorb the loss if I'm behind, I will make the call to potentially eliminate or cripple an opponent.

I am on the flop with a very short stack of 200 chips remaining in a 4700 pot after my pre-flop 4-bet. The board is A-6-6, and I hold K-10, giving me only King-high. With such a small amount of chips left relative to the pot, any bet I make or face will effectively put me all-in. There is no strategic value in betting here as I have no fold equity and am likely behind the opponent's range. Checking is the standard play to navigate the remaining chips or simply to see if the opponent checks behind.

Flashlight 4-bet pre-flop, which signifies a very strong range, likely including high pairs or strong Ace-X hands. The flop comes Ace-high, which hits a large portion of that 4-betting range. Since Flashlight has checked and has only 200 chips remaining behind in a 4700 chip pot, they are essentially committed to the hand. Betting into a range that likely contains an Ace or a pocket pair when I only have King-high is unnecessary. Checking back allows me to see the turn for free and evaluate if I can improve my hand or if the board texture changes.

With only 200 chips remaining in a 4700 pot, my stack is negligible and I am pot-committed. My King-high is clearly behind the opponent's range, especially after they called my 4-bet pre-flop. Betting my final 200 chips provides no fold equity, as the opponent will call with any hand. Checking is the only logical play to see the river, allowing me to concede or showdown my hand depending on the opponent's action, though I am effectively forced to call any bet on the river due to the massive pot odds.

On the turn 4d, Flashlight has checked again. There are 4,700 chips in the pot and Flashlight only has 200 chips remaining. Given the pre-flop 4-bet, Flashlight's range is heavily weighted towards high pocket pairs (QQ, JJ, TT) or strong Aces. While the check on an Ace-high board might suggest weakness, betting here into a player with such a small remaining stack has no fold equity; Flashlight will call with any two cards. Since I currently hold King-high and have no made hand, there is no benefit to betting. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially win at showdown if Flashlight was 4-betting a hand like KQ or a lower pocket pair that I might improve against, or simply to minimize losses if they are trapping with an Ace.

With the river completed and the board showing an Ace and a Queen, my King-high is extremely unlikely to win against Flash's range, which called my significant 4-bet pre-flop. My remaining stack of 200 chips in a 4700 pot offers zero fold equity, and betting would essentially be throwing away the last of my chips. Checking is the only rational move to see the showdown.

We are at the river on a board of 6h-6s-Ac-4d-Qd. I hold King-high (KsJs). Flashlight 4-bet pre-flop, which usually represents a strong range like high pocket pairs or big Aces. Flashlight has checked every street including the river and only has 200 chips left in a 4,700 chip pot. Since Flashlight is essentially pot-committed, a bet of 200 would have zero fold equity; they will call with any hand they chose to 4-bet. My King-high has very little showdown value against a 4-betting range, but since I cannot make them fold, there is no reason to bet. I will check back and hope to win the pot at showdown against a hand like total air or a lower King-high, while minimizing the loss if they are holding a made hand.

I am in the button position with a very weak starting hand (Jack-5 offsuit). Given my short stack size, playing speculative or weak hands is suboptimal. Folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind. I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit. Flashlight, who is the shortest stack at the table, has raised from the button. Given that the Big Blind (Pro) is still to act and could easily 3-bet, and that I want to maintain my healthy stack lead, folding this trash hand is the most prudent play.

With 87o in the Big Blind facing a 2.5x open from the Button, our hand is too weak to defend out of position. It lacks the suitedness to make strong draws and will often be dominated or face difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the standard and most +EV play here.

I am on the button with T3o. This is a very weak hand. Pro in the small blind has already posted 50, and Flashlight in the big blind has posted 100. Given the stack sizes (I have the chip lead, but Flashlight is relatively short with 3300), and my position, I could potentially raise to steal the blinds, but T3o is near the bottom of my range. However, usually, a fold or a fold-equivalent move is standard here. Since I am first to act and it's a 3-handed game, folding is the safest and most disciplined play with such a weak hand, as I don't want to get involved in a pot out of position or against a short-stack's shove.

With Q9o in a Blind vs Blind situation after the Button folds, I have a decent hand to attack the Big Blind. A standard raise to 300 puts pressure on Flashlight, whose 33 BB stack might encourage them to overfold their weak to marginal holdings, allowing me to steal the blinds or take the initiative into the flop.

I am the short stack and hold a very weak hand, Ten-Deuce suited. While the pot odds are tempting, calling against the small blind's raise with such a marginal hand out of position is negative expected value. Given my limited stack, I must conserve chips for a better opportunity.

Q8o is a weak offsuit hand. Although I am on the Button, stealing with Q8o against a short-stacked SB (32.5 BB) and a deep-stacked BB who defends often is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and safest play here.

I am the short stack and need to aggressively accumulate chips to stay competitive. In the small blind against the big blind, King-Queen offsuit is a premium hand. Raising allows me to take initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop, or at least put pressure on the big blind. A raise to 300 is a standard opening size given the table history.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh2s. Flashlight, who is in the Small Blind, has a very short stack of 3000 chips (30 BB) and has raised to 300. While Qh2s is a weak hand, the pot odds are 2:1 to call (200 to win 400). However, given Flashlight's short stack and the relative weakness of my hand, calling just to see a flop out of position might lead to difficult decisions if I hit a marginal piece. Flashlight has been active with pre-flop raises. Folding here preserves my chip lead (14,650) without risking chips on a sub-par starting hand against a potential shove-range from a short stack.

Jack-Four offsuit is a very weak hand with little potential. Given my short stack size relative to my opponents and my position on the button, it is strategically sound to conserve my chips and wait for a stronger starting hand rather than contesting this pot with a marginal holding.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 6h. The Button has folded, making it a head-up situation between me and the Big Blind. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to call. While 6-5 offsuit is a weak hand, in a blind vs. blind scenario with these stack sizes, I have the option to complete the small blind, fold, or raise. Given that Pro is a solid player and I'm out of position, and the fact that 5-6 offsuit doesn't play very well post-flop, I will choose to fold to avoid playing a weak hand out of position against a stack that can put pressure on me.

I am on the button with Ace-high (Ac 4h) in a three-handed game. Ace-high is a strong opening hand in this position. Flashlight (BB) has a relatively short stack of 3,300 chips (33 BBs), which makes them more likely to either fold or commit. I will put in a standard raise of 300 chips to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take down the pot pre-flop, or play a heads-up pot with the range advantage usually afforded to the button opener.

With A9 suited in the Small Blind facing a Button raise, we have a hand that is strong enough to play but benefits significantly from fold equity and taking the betting lead. 3-betting rather than calling mitigates our positional disadvantage post-flop, puts pressure on the Button's wide opening range, and discourages the Big Blind from squeezing with their 33 BB stack.

I am facing a 3-bet from the Small Blind after an initial raise from the Button. With pocket 4s, my hand does not play well in a bloated pot out of position. Given that I have 33 big blinds remaining, I need to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation. Calling would commit a significant portion of my chips without favorable odds for set-mining, and 4-betting is too risky against this range. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am on the button with Ac 4h. I initially opened for 300, and Pro (Small Blind) 3-bet to 1000. Flashlight (Big Blind) has folded. The action is back on me. My hand, A4o, is a marginal hand to continue with against a 3-bet from a competent player like Pro, especially when they are out of position. However, I have a massive stack lead (14,300 chips). Calling keeps the pot manageable while I have position post-flop, allowing me to see a flop and decide if I want to proceed. Folding is the safest play as A4o is easily dominated, and I want to preserve my chip lead without getting involved in a bloated pot with a weak Ace. In this specific tournament dynamic, 3-betting light is common, but calling allows me to evaluate the board. Given the stack sizes and the 'To Call' amount being 700 into a pot of 1400, I am getting 3:1 odds. While my hand is weak, I'll fold to avoid unnecessary variance and keep my focus on better spots since I am already the chip leader.

Holding Jack-Three offsuit on the Button, this is a very weak starting hand. With both opponents yet to act and one having a larger stack, there is no reason to attempt a steal. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

I am in the small blind heads-up against the big blind after the button folded. With a weak hand like J-5 offsuit, I prefer to complete the small blind to see a flop rather than folding or raising, as the pot odds (getting 2:1) justify a call.

Flashlight completed from the small blind, which typically indicates a weak range. I have Ace-high (Ad 7s), which is likely the best hand. I will raise to 300 to attempt to win the pot immediately or play a larger pot in position post-flop. Given Flashlight's short stack of 3200, if they respond with a shove, I can comfortably fold and minimize my losses.

Jh-5s is a weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive raise. Given my short stack of 3200, I cannot afford to call raises with marginal holdings that are likely dominated. Folding is the disciplined choice here to preserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with Jh 8h, a suited hand that has some playability in position. Given my short stack relative to the other players, I need to be proactive in picking up pots. Raising to 250 allows me to potentially take down the blinds pre-flop or see a flop with initiative, which is a necessary strategic move to rebuild my stack.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Seven offsuit is a strong hand against a button opening range. Flashlight is currently the short stack with under 30 big blinds. By 3-betting to 850, I am putting pressure on the button to either fold or commit a significant portion of their stack. This action also serves to discourage Pro in the Big Blind from entering the pot with marginal hands. Given the stack sizes and the 3-handed nature of the game, I am comfortable playing for Flashlight's remaining chips if they choose to shove.

Facing an open from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, Pocket Eights is in a tough spot. Calling is unprofitable because we are out of position, do not close the pre-flop action (Flashlight has a short stack and could back-jam), and we don't have the necessary implied odds to set-mine given the large 3-bet sizing. 4-betting as a bluff or for value is overplaying our hand against the Small Blind's strong 3-betting range. The safest and most standard play is to fold.

I am on the button with suited connectors (Jh 8h), which have some playability, but facing a significant 3-bet from the small blind. With a short stack of 2950, calling 600 chips (a large portion of my remaining stack) to play post-flop out of position or in a bloated pot without a premium hand is mathematically suboptimal and high-variance. My opponent's 3-bet range from the small blind is likely strong. I should fold here to preserve my remaining chips and wait for a better opportunity to commit my stack with higher equity.

In the current position as the Button, I have Q2o, a relatively weak hand. The pot is small (150) and it costs me 100 to call the big blind of 100. Historically, I have seen 'Pro' and 'Flashlight' being somewhat active, with Flashlight having a short stack (2850). Given the overall goal is to maximize stack size and the weakness of this hand, folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips for better opportunities.

With Q3o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a very weak offsuit hand. The Big Blind has a somewhat short stack of around 28.5 BBs and might defend aggressively or shove if I try to steal. Folding is the safest and most standard play to preserve my chip stack.

Q6o is a very weak and disconnected hand. Even though I am on the Button, the hand does not possess enough equity or playability to justify a raise to steal the blinds, especially with the Small Blind sitting at around 30 BBs, a stack size perfect for 3-bet shoving over wide button opens. Holding suited Ace-Four, which is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, I will raise to apply pressure on the big blind and potentially take down the pot immediately, or proceed with a positional advantage given my stack size.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 8d. The Button has folded, and the Small Blind (Flashlight) has raised to 250. Flashlight is short-stacked with only about 27.5 big blinds remaining. I have the nut flush draw on a flop with two clubs and a nine. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and I am out of position, checking is an option, but betting allows me to take down the pot now or build equity if called. A standard semi-bluff bet of half the pot (250) is appropriate here to put pressure on the big blind's wide range and realize my equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc 8d on a 9d 7c 5c flop. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has bet 250 into a 500 pot. I have a gutshot straight draw to the six and one overcard. The pot odds are 3:1, meaning I need 25% equity to call. With 4 outs for the straight and potentially 3 outs for the Jack, my raw equity is around 16-28%. Given that I am in position and Flashlight is relatively short-stacked, calling is the most appropriate action to see a turn card and potentially realize my equity or take the pot away if they check the turn.

The board is 9d 7c 5c 2s. I hold Ad 4d, giving me only Ace-high. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and my flop continuation bet was called, checking is the most prudent play here to control the pot size and avoid over-committing with a weak holding out of position. I will look to see a free river card if possible.

Flashlight's check on the turn suggests weakness or a marginal hand. However, with their relatively short stack (2500 remaining), they may be looking for a cheap showdown with a small pair or might be unwilling to fold to a bluff. I have a gutshot straight draw to the six and one overcard (the Jack). By checking back, I realize my equity for free and can potentially improve on the river without the risk of being forced off the hand by a check-shove.

With a pair of Aces after the river, I have a decent value hand, but the flush draw completed on the board. Betting now risks getting called only by better hands, such as a flush or two-pair, while potentially folding out hands I currently beat. Checking is the most prudent play to reach showdown, control the pot size, and potentially induce a bluff from an opponent who missed their draw.

The board is 9d 7c 5c 2s Ac. I have Jc 8d, giving me no pair and no showdown value. Flashlight (SB) raised pre-flop, bet the flop, and then checked both the turn and the river. This line often suggests a marginal pair or a missed hand. While the Ace on the river is a good card to bluff, Flashlight's stack is relatively short (2500 chips remaining), making them more likely to make a 'crying call' with any small pair or even a King-high that beats me. Since I am the chip leader and have zero showdown value, I will check back and concede the pot rather than attempting a high-risk bluff.

My hand (6c, 9d) is too weak to play, especially with my relatively short stack of 3500. I need to conserve my chips and wait for a more premium holding to attempt to double up or steal the blinds. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically correct play in this position.

I am in the Small Blind with Q5o. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Pro). Completing the small blind (calling the 50) is a cheap way to see a flop, but folding is also a safe option. Given my stack size and the desire to maintain a solid image, I will simply fold this hand and wait for a better spot, especially since Pro has been active.

I am on the button with 3d-5c, which is a very weak hand. The stack sizes show Flashlight is quite short with 3400 chips. With A7o in the Small Blind after the Button folds, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise and attempt to steal the Big Blind. Raising to 3x (300) puts pressure on Flashlight's shorter stack (34BB) while giving us a chance to win the pot uncontested.

With a short stack of 3400 and being in the Big Blind, I am facing a raise to 300 from the Small Blind (Pro). My hand, Ace-Five offsuit, is a standard defending hand against a Small Blind open. With Ace-high and no strong draws on a Jack-high board with a flush draw, our hand has very little equity if called.
