Checking is the most prudent action here to control the size of the pot and evaluate our opponent's response.

I have bottom pair with an Ace kicker on a board that contains a flush draw and potential straight draws. Pro checked the flop, which could indicate a weak hand or a slow-played monster. Given my short stack, I want to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity. Checking back is the most prudent play to see the turn without risking more chips unnecessarily.

With Flashlight checking behind on the flop, their range likely consists of marginal pairs, draws, or high cards. The 6 on the turn gives us a gutshot straight draw to go with our Ace high. While a delayed continuation bet might fold out some better Ace-highs or small pairs, checking allows us to control the pot size and potentially see a free river. Betting into a shorter stack is somewhat risky as they might jam their draws, forcing us to fold our equity. Checking is a safe and solid approach to realize our hand's potential.

I am in the Big Blind with a pair of 5s on a board of Jh-3h-5s-6d. Pro checked the flop and has now checked the turn. With a stack of 3200, I want to preserve my remaining chips. Betting into the opponent is risky as it might isolate me against better hands, such as a Jack or higher pair, and I cannot call a raise. Checking behind allows me to control the pot size and reach a showdown cheaply, which is the most prudent strategy given the current state of the board and my stack size.

The river brings a Ten, and all the obvious draws (like heart flush draws or straight draws) have missed. Our Ace-high has reasonable showdown value against those busted draws, but we cannot realistically value bet since worse hands will fold and we will only be called by a pair or better. Attempting to bluff is unnecessary and risky, as Flashlight might bluff-catch with a small pair that checked down. Checking to realize our showdown value or safely fold to a large bet is the optimal play.

I am holding a pair of fives (As-5d) on a board of Jh-3h-5s-6d-Ts. Betting here is unlikely to gain value as most hands that call will beat my pair of fives, and I risk losing more chips with a short stack if I get check-raised. Checking behind allows me to take my hand to a cheap showdown and preserve my remaining 3200 stack.

I am on the Button with 2s 3d, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With 3 players left and no reason to get involved in the pot with such a weak holding, the optimal move is to simply fold.

The button folded, and I am in the small blind with 7-10 offsuit. Since I have already posted 50 and need only 50 more to complete the blind and see a flop against the big blind, this is a standard play to see a cheap flop in a late position scenario despite the weak cards.

I am in the Big Blind holding JTo. The Small Blind (Flashlight) has completed the blind. I am in the small blind with 7-10 offsuit, a weak, disconnected hand. The big blind, Flash, has raised to 350. Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, Queen-Eight suited is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. The standard play here is to fold and preserve our stack, avoiding dominated situations.

I am on the Button with A7o, which is a solid hand to open-raise with to steal the blinds. Having an Ace blocks premium hands from my opponents, and playing in position will give me a post-flop advantage if called. A standard raise to 250 applies good pressure.

My hand (7d Td) is quite weak for playing out of position against a button raise. Given that my stack (3950) is significantly shorter than both opponents, chip preservation is paramount. I am in the Big Blind with K-high (Kh 3d). The button (Pro) has raised to 250. I am on the button with King-Deuce suited. While this hand has some potential for a steal in a 3-handed game, it is a weak hand and I am currently short-stacked relative to my opponents (40 BB). J5o is a very weak and easily dominated hand. Even though it is blind versus blind, calling a 3x raise with this hand out of position post-flop (if I call I'll have position, actually, since SB acts first post-flop, but still) or facing a 3x raise pre-flop makes this a clear fold.

I am on the button with Qh Ts, which is a solid hand to open for a raise in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy, and the blinds have been folding to button aggression recently. Ten-Seven offsuit is a very weak hand to play from the Small Blind facing a raise from the Button. Folding is the standard and mathematically correct play here to preserve my stack.

Holding King-Queen offsuit in the big blind against a button raise is a standard calling hand, especially with a short stack of 3850. I am in the big blind with K-Q high on a board of A-9-9. This board is extremely unfavorable for my hand as it hits the pre-flop raiser's range (which includes many Aces) much harder than mine. With no pair and no draw, I have very little equity. I raised pre-flop with Qh Ts and was called by the Big Blind (Flashlight). The flop comes 9c 9s Ad. The flop is [9c, 9s, Ad], which is extremely favorable for the pre-flop aggressor (Flash), who likely holds many Aces or nines in their range. I'm on the Button with 4-2 offsuit, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. I will definitely fold and wait for a better opportunity to get involved, especially with the chip leader in the Big Blind.

I am in the Small Blind with suited connectors (Jd Td) against the Big Blind after the Button folded. With a stack of 3600 (36 BBs), I have enough chips to play aggressively. Raising to 300 is a standard move to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or gain initiative in a heads-up situation.

I hold A9o in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Flashlight), who is playing a relatively short stack of approximately 33.5 BB, has raised to 300. I am in the Small Blind with suited connectors (Jd Td), facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind (Flash). The current pot is 1200, and I need to call 600 more. This provides good pot odds (33%) to see a flop. While I am out of position, Jd Td has good equity against a wide 3-betting range and performs well in multi-street play. Given my stack size of 3350, calling keeps the pot manageable, whereas a 4-bet shove might be too high-variance against an aggressive opponent who covers me significantly. The flop of 2c 6c 5s is somewhat connected, but I have completely missed with Jack-Ten offsuit. I am out of position against the aggressor who 3-bet pre-flop. With no pair and no strong draw, I have minimal equity. Checking is the prudent play to see how my opponent reacts. If they make a standard continuation bet, I will be prepared to fold as the hand has little potential to improve against a strong range.

I 3-bet pre-flop and was called by Flashlight. The flop comes 2c-6c-5s, which is quite low and potentially dangerous for my 3-betting range, although I still hold Ace-high. Flashlight has checked. Given that Flashlight is playing a relatively short stack (approximately 1.5 SPR), a bet here might commit me to a shove from him. Checking back allows me to see a turn card for free, realize my equity with overcards, and maintain pot control against a range that likely includes some middle pairs or small connectors.

The turn has paired my Ten, giving me top pair with a decent kicker. My opponent checked the flop, which caps their range significantly and suggests they might be holding overcards, a weak pair, or a draw. I am out of position and want to extract value from weaker hands like 6x, 5x, or middle pairs that might have checked back the flop. A bet of 700 into an 1800 pot (nearly 40%) is a standard value bet to build the pot and potentially get called by worse hands while still being small enough to fold if I face a significant raise, acknowledging the potential flush draw on the board.

On the turn, the board is 2c-6c-5s-Th. I hold Ace-high with A9o. Pre-flop, I 3-bet to 900 and was called. The flop was checked through. Flashlight's lead for 700 into a pot of 1800 (about 39% pot) is a common probe bet after the aggressor (me) checks back the flop. Given the pot odds (700 to call for a total pot of 3200, requiring about 22% equity) and the fact that I still have two overcards and potentially the best hand against missed draws like 87 or clubs, folding is too tight. Raising is risky against a short stack who is likely pot-committed. I will call to see the river and evaluate.

The river is the Eight of Diamonds, which does not change the board significantly and leaves me with a pair of Tens. My opponent 3-bet pre-flop, checked back the flop, and called my turn bet. This line suggests they might have a weak made hand (like a weak pair or A-high) or missed a draw. My hand is a clear bluff-catcher; I am unlikely to get called by worse hands if I bet, and betting would only fold out air while isolating myself against hands that beat me. Checking is the most prudent decision to reach showdown, allowing me to win against bluffs and minimize loss against better hands.

On the river 8d, the board is 2c-6c-5s-Th-8d. Flashlight has checked after leading on the turn. My Ace-high has some showdown value, especially since the missed club draw and several straight draws (like 43 or 74) are in Flashlight's range. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands or to fold out better hands, especially given Flashlight's remaining stack size of 2050. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and take the hand to showdown without risking more chips against a range that might have improved to a pair of 8s or was already ahead with a 10.

With 9-8 offsuit in the button position and a short stack of 5250, this is a marginal hand that does not warrant an investment. My stack size is becoming critical compared to the opponents, so I need to prioritize stronger holdings or a clear opportunity to steal the blinds. I hold pocket fives in the Big Blind facing a standard button open from Flashlight, who has a relatively short stack of about 32 big blinds. Calling 200 to win 450 gives me good pot odds, and the implied odds for set-mining against a 30bb stack are sufficient. A 3-bet could induce an all-in from Flashlight, which would put 55 in a tough spot, so flatting is the most profitable and lowest-variance play here.

I have pocket fives on a T-9-4 flop. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser, the standard approach is to check and evaluate his continuation bet. Donk betting here accomplishes little, as better hands will call or raise, and worse hands will fold.

The flop of Ten-Four-Nine is somewhat coordinated, and I have only King-Queen high with no made hand or significant draw. Betting here would likely be a pure bluff into a player who already showed strength by defending their big blind. Given my short stack and the board texture, checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and avoid bloating a pot against a wider defending range.

With the Queen hitting the turn, the board now has three overcards to my pocket fives. Since Flashlight checked behind on the flop, his range still contains plenty of broadway hands that just improved, as well as mid-pairs that beat me. Betting 55 here would only get called by better hands, turning it into a bluff with low equity. Checking allows me to control the pot size and evaluate his action, with the intention of folding to a bet.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a board that is somewhat connected but unlikely to have hit the big blind's checking range significantly. Betting 350 into the 650 pot allows me to charge potential draws like J-x or extract value from weaker hands like 10-x or 9-x while maintaining some stack preservation if I face aggression.

Facing a turn bet on a board with three overcards (Queen, Ten, Nine) to my pocket fives, my hand is severely downgraded. My opponent's delayed continuation bet or turn stab hits his flop checking range well, which includes broadway cards that just paired the Queen or picked up draws (like KJ/J8). I have essentially no equity against value bets and am even vulnerable to bluffs with overcards. Following my turn plan, the most sensible action is to fold.

On the Button in a 3-handed game with a stack of 11,600, 6h 5d is a marginal speculative hand. I have pocket Aces (Ah Ac) on the Button, the strongest possible starting hand. My primary goal is to build a large pot and extract maximum value. I am in the Big Blind with J3o. Pro, who has been an active opener from the button, has raised to 300. To call, it costs me 200 more into a pot of 450. I am holding a very weak hand (4-9 offsuit) on the button. I am in the Small Blind with 9h7h, a solid suited gapper that plays well post-flop. Since the Button folded, I am heads-up against the Big Blind (Pro). Raising here allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate for this situation.

With King-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind facing a Small Blind open, we have a strong hand that plays very well in position post-flop. On a board of Qs-Js-6d, the texture significantly favors a Big Blind calling range which often contains many Broadway cards. I have 9-high with only a very thin backdoor straight draw. With middle pair and a strong kicker on a wet board (Qs Js 6d), checking behind is the best play for pot control. Betting could fold out weaker hands we are currently beating while isolating ourselves against stronger hands (like top pair, two pair, or overpairs) and aggressive semi-bluffs that could force us to fold a decent hand. By checking, we realize our equity, keep the opponent's range wide, and allow them to bluff on the turn.

The turn Ten of Clubs provides me with an open-ended straight draw (needing a King or an Eight). Since I checked the flop and Pro checked behind, neither of us has shown significant strength. By checking again, I aim to see a free river card to potentially complete my straight. If Pro bets, I will evaluate the price, but with an open-ended draw in a relatively small pot, checking is the most prudent way to realize my equity without over-committing against the chip leader.

With middle pair and an open-ended straight draw after the turn brings a Ten, our equity has improved significantly. The opponent has checked twice, indicating a marginal hand or a draw. Betting here serves as a semi-bluff and for protection, denying equity to overcards and flush draws while building the pot in case we hit our straight.

On the turn, the Ten of Clubs gives me an open-ended straight draw (any King or Eight completes it). Pro has bet 300 into a 600 pot after both of us checked the flop. With an 8-out draw, my immediate pot odds are roughly 25% (300 to call a 1200 total pot) while my equity to hit on the river is approximately 17.4%. While the immediate odds are slightly insufficient, the implied odds are significant if I hit my straight, especially since Pro is the chip leader and likely to pay off a well-disguised straight. Calling is the most efficient way to realize my equity and see the river card.

On the river, the board is JACK OF SPADES (Js) QUEEN OF SPADES (Qs) SIX OF DIAMONDS (6d) TEN OF CLUBS (Tc) ACE OF HEARTS (Ah). I am holding SEVEN OF HEARTS (7h) NINE OF HEARTS (9h), giving me nothing but 9-high. On the turn, I had a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8), which did not materialize. The board is now extremely coordinated; any King makes a broadway straight (T-J-Q-K-A), and any 8 makes a lower straight (8-9-T-J-Q). Given the pre-flop action where I raised and Pro called from the Big Blind, both of us could easily have a King or an 8 in our ranges. Since I have zero showdown value and Pro is the chip leader, bluffing into this board texture is highly risky. My best course of action is to check and, if Pro bets, fold to preserve my stack for the remaining hands, especially since Flashlight is short-stacked and outlasting them is a priority.

The river brings an Ace, giving me the Broadway nut straight (A-K-Q-J-T). Since there are no flush possibilities on the board, I have the absolute best hand. My objective is to extract maximum value. The opponent's check-call on the turn and check on the river suggest they might have caught top pair with the Ace, hit two pair, or have a weaker straight (like K9). A value bet of around 2/3 to 3/4 of the pot should be the perfect size to get called by these hands.

I am holding 9-high on a board that is extremely coordinated (Js-Qs-6d-Tc-Ah). Any King makes the Nut Straight, and any 8 makes a lower straight. With a very weak hand like Jack-Three offsuit, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot, especially since I am playing short-stacked with only two hands remaining in the tournament. Folding preserves my current stack.

Holding 9c 4s in the small blind facing a 3x raise from the button, my hand is far too weak and disconnected to play. Folding is the only viable option here to preserve my stack.
